questions.question: You need to grant a teammate the lowest workspace role that still lets them onboard additional collaborators into the Viewer and Contributor roles for that workspace, without giving them the ability to delete the workspace or add other admins. Which role do you assign?

options.A: Member
options.B: Admin
options.C: Contributor
options.D: Viewer

questions.explanation: Member is the least-privileged role that can add other people to the workspace at Member level or lower, but cannot delete the workspace or add admins. Contributor can create and write items but cannot add any users, so it fails the onboarding requirement; Admin works but is more than the minimum asked for.

questions.question: Raw CSV files sit in an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 container owned by a partner team. Your lakehouse must expose those files as Delta-compatible tables that the SQL analytics endpoint can read, but your storage bill must not grow and you must not run a recurring job to keep the files in sync. Which approach meets all of these constraints?

options.A: A Dataflow Gen2 with a Lakehouse table destination on a daily schedule
options.B: A OneLake shortcut that targets the ADLS Gen2 path
options.C: A mirrored database pointed at the storage account
options.D: A Copy activity in a pipeline triggered when new blobs arrive

questions.explanation: A OneLake shortcut surfaces the partner data inside the lakehouse and exposes it to the SQL analytics endpoint while leaving the bytes in ADLS Gen2, so no extra storage is consumed and nothing must be re-synced. Mirroring is the strongest distractor, but it continuously replicates into OneLake (consuming storage) and targets operational databases, not a flat-file container.

questions.question:
You run the following against a Fabric warehouse:

```sql
COPY INTO dbo.Sales
FROM 'https://account.blob.core.windows.net/data/sales/*.parquet'
WITH (FILE_TYPE = 'PARQUET', CREDENTIAL = (IDENTITY = 'Managed Identity'));
```

The dbo.Sales table already exists and already contains last quarter's rows. Which statement about the result is correct?

options.A: The existing rows are truncated and replaced by the files matching the wildcard
options.B: The statement fails because the column list and ERRORFILE arguments are required
options.C: The matching files are appended to dbo.Sales in a single parallelized bulk load
options.D: The statement fails because COPY INTO cannot target a table that already has data

questions.explanation: COPY INTO is an append-style bulk loader: it reads every file matching the wildcard in parallel and adds the rows to the existing table without removing what is already there. It does not truncate or replace the existing rows, and neither a column list nor an ERRORFILE argument is mandatory, so the load succeeds rather than failing.

questions.question: Your development workspace is connected to an Azure DevOps repo through Git integration, and a three-stage deployment pipeline (Dev, Test, Prod) is configured. A notebook in Dev references a lakehouse; in Prod it must instead point at the production lakehouse. After committing the notebook change, which TWO actions correctly propagate the work to Prod with the right binding?

options.A: Define a deployment rule on the Prod stage that sets the notebook's default lakehouse
options.B: Deploy directly from the Git repo into Prod, bypassing the Test stage
options.C: Promote the content Dev to Test, then Test to Prod through the deployment pipeline
options.D: Add a parameter rule on the notebook so the lakehouse path is rewritten at commit time
options.E: Connect the Prod workspace's Git to the Dev branch so it always mirrors Dev

questions.explanation: Git integration is the source-control layer; promotion to Prod is done stage-by-stage through the deployment pipeline (promoting Dev to Test, then Test to Prod), and a per-stage deployment rule on the Prod stage is what rebinds the notebook's default lakehouse to the production lakehouse. Deploying straight from Git into Prod and bypassing Test breaks stage progression, a parameter rule does not apply to notebooks (notebooks support a default-lakehouse rule, not a parameter rule), and pointing Prod's Git at the Dev branch defeats stage isolation.

questions.question: After heavy streaming ingestion, a lakehouse Delta table is fragmented into thousands of tiny Parquet files and accumulating obsolete files. As scheduled maintenance you want to first consolidate the live data into fewer, larger read-optimized files and then reclaim storage by purging files no longer referenced. Which TWO commands accomplish this maintenance, run in that order?

options.A: ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS
options.B: OPTIMIZE
options.C: REORG TABLE ... APPLY (PURGE)
options.D: VACUUM

questions.explanation: OPTIMIZE first bin-compacts the many small files into larger, V-Order-optimized files for fast reads, and VACUUM then removes the now-unreferenced files to reclaim storage; running VACUUM after OPTIMIZE is the recommended order. REORG TABLE only rewrites files to materialize deletion-vector deletes and does not consolidate the live data, and ANALYZE TABLE merely refreshes statistics rather than touching the physical files.

questions.question: A retailer ingests clickstream events from Azure Event Hubs into an Eventstream, applies a filter operator to drop test events, and now needs to fan the surviving events out to persistent destinations: one for ad-hoc KQL analysis and one for next-morning batch reports in the lakehouse. Which TWO destinations can the Eventstream route directly to in this scenario?

options.A: A Fabric warehouse table via T-SQL INSERT
options.B: A Power BI semantic model import table
options.C: An Eventhouse (KQL database)
options.D: A lakehouse

questions.explanation: An Eventstream can fan out to multiple destinations, including an Eventhouse/KQL database for real-time KQL analysis and a lakehouse for batch reporting, all from the no-code editor. Eventstream does not write directly into a semantic model import table or run T-SQL INSERTs into a warehouse, and it cannot push to an on-premises SQL Server as a destination.

questions.question:
Against a KQL table named Events that has columns Timestamp, DeviceId, and Status, you run:

```kusto
Events
| where Timestamp > ago(1h)
| summarize arg_max(Timestamp, Status) by DeviceId
```

What does the query return?

options.A: For each DeviceId, the earliest Status recorded in the last hour
options.B: A count of events per DeviceId over the last hour
options.C: The single most recent event across all devices in the last hour
options.D: For each DeviceId, the Status from that device's most recent event in the last hour

questions.explanation: arg_max(Timestamp, Status) returns the Status value from the row with the maximum Timestamp within each DeviceId group, i.e. the latest Status per device in the window. It is not a count (that would be count()), and it is per device rather than a single global row; arg_min would give the earliest.

questions.question: A warehouse table dbo.Customers stores full email addresses. Support staff should see results where the address renders as a masked value such as aXXX@XXXX.com, but the real values must stay intact in storage and be visible to privileged roles. Which feature implements this with the least change to existing queries?

options.A: Revoking column SELECT permission on the Email column
options.B: A view that applies LEFT() and CONCAT() to the Email column
options.C: Encrypting the Email column at rest
options.D: A row-level security filter predicate on dbo.Customers
options.E: Dynamic data masking on the Email column

questions.explanation: Dynamic data masking applies a masking function (the email mask gives aXXX@XXXX.com) at query time for unprivileged users while leaving the stored value unchanged and the existing query text the same. Revoking column permission (a form of column-level security) blocks the column entirely rather than masking it, and a custom view would force every consumer to query the view instead of the table.

questions.question: A streaming pipeline writes to a Delta table every few seconds, producing many small files. You will run OPTIMIZE periodically, but you also want to cut the number of small files created at write time so each micro-batch emits appropriately sized files. Which TWO settings address the small-file problem at write time?

options.A: Increase delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration to 30 days
options.B: Set delta.logRetentionDuration to a shorter interval
options.C: Enable auto compaction (autoCompact) for the table
options.D: Enable optimized write (optimizeWrite) on the table

questions.explanation: Optimized write coalesces data within a write so each commit emits fewer, larger files, and auto compaction runs a small compaction after a write to merge tiny files automatically; together they attack the problem at write time. The retention settings only govern how long old files or log entries survive for time travel and have no effect on small-file creation.

questions.question: A governance lead asks how Fabric attaches compute and how Purview sensitivity labels behave once applied. Which statement is accurate?

options.A: Compute is assigned per user session, and sensitivity labels are managed exclusively in Power BI Desktop
options.B: Compute is assigned per workspace, and a sensitivity label set on an item can propagate to downstream derived items
options.C: Compute is assigned per item, and a sensitivity label set on a report propagates upstream to its sources
options.D: Compute is assigned per workspace, and sensitivity labels can only be set at the tenant level

questions.explanation: A capacity is bound at the workspace level so every item inherits that compute, and Purview sensitivity labels are applied per item and flow downstream to content derived from a labeled source. Label inheritance moves downstream (toward consumers); it does not propagate upstream to a report's sources, and labels are not confined to Power BI Desktop or set only at the tenant level.

questions.question:
You run this PySpark in a Fabric notebook against a Delta table that contains the values 'shipped', 'Shipped', and 'pending' in the status column:

```python
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')
result = (df.filter(df.status == 'shipped')
            .groupBy('region')
            .agg({'amount': 'sum'}))
result.show()
```

Which statement best describes the output?

options.A: Sum of amount per region for both 'shipped' and 'Shipped' rows
options.B: An error, because agg requires a Column expression rather than a dict
options.C: Sum of amount per region for rows where status is exactly 'shipped' (case-sensitive)
options.D: Count of rows per region regardless of status

questions.explanation: Spark string equality is case-sensitive by default, so the filter matches only 'shipped' and excludes 'Shipped'; the result is sum(amount) grouped by region. The dictionary form of agg is valid syntax, and groupBy keeps one row per region rather than collapsing to a single total.

questions.question: You want a no-code object that watches a metric flowing through an Eventstream and, when a threshold is crossed, sends a Teams message and kicks off a pipeline run. Which item provides this?

options.A: A Real-Time Dashboard
options.B: A Real-Time hub subscription
options.C: A KQL materialized view
options.D: An Activator

questions.explanation: Activator monitors data from Eventstreams, KQL queries, or Power BI and fires actions such as Teams or email alerts and pipeline starts when conditions are met, all without code. A dashboard only visualizes, a materialized view pre-aggregates data, and the Real-Time hub is a discovery surface for streams rather than a rule engine.

questions.question: In a Fabric warehouse you must build a new FactSales table by joining a staging table to existing dimensions to resolve surrogate keys, in one set-based pass that also defines the new table's structure. Which approach fits best?

options.A: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT that joins staging to the dimensions
options.B: INSERT INTO an empty, pre-created FactSales from a SELECT with the joins
options.C: A MERGE from staging into a pre-created FactSales
options.D: A Dataflow Gen2 that performs the dimension lookups

questions.explanation: CTAS both defines and populates the table from a query in a single parallelized operation, which is the canonical Fabric warehouse pattern for building a fact table from joined sources. INSERT...SELECT and MERGE both require the table to already exist, so they are not one-step table creation, and a Dataflow Gen2 is a separate low-code engine rather than the requested set-based T-SQL pass.

questions.question: Users report that a Fabric warehouse occasionally slows down. Using only built-in T-SQL, you must identify which historical queries ran longest and which were run most frequently over recent days. Which object set do you query?

options.A: sys.query_store_runtime_stats
options.B: The queryinsights schema views, such as long_running_queries
options.C: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.ROUTINES
options.D: sys.dm_exec_requests joined to sys.dm_exec_sql_text

questions.explanation: The Fabric warehouse exposes the queryinsights schema (long_running_queries, frequently_run_queries, exec_requests_history) specifically for historical query performance analysis in T-SQL. The sys.dm_exec_* DMVs only show currently executing or very recent activity, and Query Store is not the surfaced mechanism in the Fabric warehouse.

questions.question: You connect to a lakehouse's SQL analytics endpoint and to a Fabric warehouse from the same SQL client. Which TWO statements about the difference between them are true?

options.A: Only the SQL analytics endpoint accepts T-SQL SELECT statements
options.B: The warehouse supports CREATE TABLE, while the SQL analytics endpoint cannot create base tables
options.C: The SQL analytics endpoint can run stored procedures that modify data, the warehouse cannot
options.D: Only the warehouse can enforce row-level security
options.E: The warehouse supports INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, while the SQL analytics endpoint cannot modify data

questions.explanation: The warehouse is a full read/write T-SQL engine, so it supports DDL (CREATE TABLE) and DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE); the lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint is read-only over Delta tables, so it can neither create base tables nor modify data. Both surfaces accept SELECT statements and both can enforce row-level security, so the claims that only the endpoint accepts SELECT, that the endpoint can run data-modifying procedures, and that only the warehouse can enforce row-level security are all false.

questions.question:
A Fabric warehouse has this security setup:

```sql
CREATE FUNCTION dbo.fn_regionfilter(@Region AS sysname)
RETURNS TABLE WITH SCHEMABINDING
AS RETURN SELECT 1 AS allow
WHERE @Region = USER_NAME() OR USER_NAME() = 'manager';

CREATE SECURITY POLICY dbo.RegionPolicy
ADD FILTER PREDICATE dbo.fn_regionfilter(Region) ON dbo.Sales
WITH (STATE = ON);
```

An analyst whose USER_NAME() returns 'east' runs SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dbo.Sales. What happens?

options.A: The count includes only rows where Region = 'east'
options.B: The query is blocked with a permission error
options.C: The Region column returns NULL but all rows are counted
options.D: Only the Region column is hidden; the count is unaffected
options.E: The count includes every row in dbo.Sales

questions.explanation: A FILTER PREDICATE implements row-level security: the predicate returns a row only when Region equals the caller's name (or the caller is 'manager'), so the analyst silently sees and counts only the Region = 'east' rows. RLS filters rows transparently rather than raising a permission error, nulling out the Region column, or hiding only the column while counting every row; the 'manager' user would see all rows, but 'east' does not.

questions.question: In a data pipeline, a Copy activity ingests data. A Notebook activity must run only when the Copy succeeds, and a separate alert step must run only when the Copy fails. Which dependency configuration on the Copy activity is correct?

options.A: Completion path to both the Notebook and the alert
options.B: Completion path to the Notebook; Success path to the alert
options.C: Failure path to the Notebook; Success path to the alert
options.D: Success path to the Notebook; Failure path to the alert

questions.explanation: Wiring the Notebook on the Copy activity's Success path runs it only after a successful Copy, and wiring the alert on the Failure path runs it only when the Copy fails. A Completion path fires regardless of outcome, so any configuration that uses Completion would launch its downstream step on both success and failure, which violates the requirement; mapping the Notebook to Failure and the alert to Success would also invert the intended behavior.

questions.question: Which statement correctly characterizes OneLake in Microsoft Fabric?

options.A: It is one logical data lake per tenant, storing data in open Delta/Parquet that all workloads share
options.B: It persists data in a proprietary format that requires export before external tools can read it
options.C: It stores Power BI semantic models but lakehouse and warehouse data live in separate storage
options.D: Each workspace receives an isolated lake that cannot be referenced by other workspaces

questions.explanation: OneLake is provisioned once per tenant as a single logical lake, holds data in open Delta/Parquet, and underpins every Fabric workload so one copy of data serves many engines. Workspaces are organizational folders within that single lake (not isolated lakes), and lakehouse and warehouse data both live in OneLake.

questions.question: An operational Azure SQL Database must be reflected in OneLake as Delta tables in near real time so analysts can query it alongside other Fabric data, with essentially no ETL to build or maintain. Which capability is purpose-built for this?

options.A: A OneLake shortcut to the Azure SQL Database
options.B: Dataflow Gen2 with incremental refresh on a schedule
options.C: An Eventstream with a database change source
options.D: Fabric mirroring of the Azure SQL Database
options.E: A pipeline running COPY INTO every minute

questions.explanation: Mirroring continuously replicates a supported operational database (Azure SQL DB, Cosmos DB, Snowflake, and others) into OneLake as Delta tables in near real time with no ETL to author. A shortcut cannot target a transactional SQL database as a Delta source, and scheduled Dataflow/COPY INTO would be custom, latency-bound ETL.

questions.question:
In a KQL database you define:

```kusto
.create materialized-view DailySales on table Sales
{
    Sales
    | summarize TotalAmount = sum(Amount) by bin(Timestamp, 1d)
}
```

Which statement about this materialized view is correct?

options.A: It is recomputed in full on every query against the view
options.B: It physically replaces the Sales table to reclaim storage
options.C: It is maintained incrementally as new rows are ingested into Sales
options.D: It must be refreshed manually after each ingestion batch

questions.explanation: A KQL materialized view incrementally folds newly ingested source rows into the stored aggregate, so queries read pre-aggregated results without a full recomputation or any manual refresh. It coexists with the source table (it does not replace Sales) and continues to reflect data ingested after creation.

questions.question: You are layering a lakehouse with bronze, silver, and gold zones. Incoming files are preserved untouched in bronze; curated, aggregated subject-area marts live in gold. In which zone do you deduplicate records, enforce data types, and standardize column names?

options.A: Gold
options.B: A separate staging zone outside the medallion layers
options.C: Bronze
options.D: Silver

questions.explanation: Silver is the cleansing and conforming layer where deduplication, type enforcement, and column standardization occur, producing a validated single source for downstream marts. Bronze deliberately keeps raw data unaltered for fidelity, and gold holds business-ready aggregates rather than the cleansing step.

questions.question: As part of CI/CD, an administrator must script the creation of workspaces, list existing ones, and bind each new workspace to a specific capacity. Which interface supports this automation?

options.A: The Fabric REST APIs
options.B: T-SQL DDL executed against the tenant
options.C: The semantic-link Python library in a notebook
options.D: KQL management (.create) commands

questions.explanation: The Fabric REST APIs (with Power BI REST APIs for some operations) expose workspace, item, and capacity-assignment operations for automated provisioning and CI/CD. T-SQL DDL and KQL .create commands operate inside data items, not at the workspace/capacity management layer.

questions.question: You want to read a Delta table as it existed at a specific past wall-clock time in a Fabric notebook. Which read is correct?

options.A: spark.read.format('parquet').option('timestampAsOf', '2025-01-01').load('Tables/orders')
options.B: spark.read.format('delta').option('timestampAsOf', '2025-01-01 00:00:00').load('Tables/orders')
options.C: spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', '2025-01-01').load('Tables/orders')
options.D: spark.read.format('delta').option('asOfTimestamp', '2025-01-01').load('Tables/orders')

questions.explanation: Point-in-time Delta time travel uses the timestampAsOf option with a timestamp string. versionAsOf expects an integer version number, not a date; asOfTimestamp is not a valid option name; and reading as parquet bypasses the Delta log so time travel is impossible.

questions.question: You need one place to see the status, duration, and run history of pipeline runs, dataflow refreshes, Spark applications, and semantic model refreshes across a workspace. Which Fabric experience provides this cross-workload view?

options.A: The Real-Time hub
options.B: The Capacity Metrics app
options.C: The Monitoring hub
options.D: Azure Monitor Log Analytics

questions.explanation: The Monitoring hub aggregates run status, duration, and history across Fabric item types (pipelines, dataflows, Spark jobs, dataset refreshes). The Capacity Metrics app reports capacity-unit consumption rather than per-run activity, and the Real-Time hub is for discovering streaming sources.

questions.question: Tables in an on-premises SQL Server behind a corporate firewall must be ingested into a Fabric lakehouse on a nightly schedule. You have already installed and registered an on-premises data gateway. Which TWO Fabric items can use that gateway connection to read the on-premises source?

options.A: Database mirroring of the on-premises SQL Server
options.B: A data pipeline Copy activity
options.C: A Dataflow Gen2
options.D: A OneLake shortcut to the SQL Server instance
options.E: An Eventstream with the SQL Server as a streaming source

questions.explanation: Both a data pipeline Copy activity and a Dataflow Gen2 can connect to an on-premises source through an on-premises data gateway connection and run on a schedule. A OneLake shortcut cannot target an on-premises SQL Server, mirroring of on-premises SQL Server is not supported in this way, and Eventstream does not consume an on-premises SQL table as a streaming source through the gateway.

questions.question: A Spark notebook join runs for an hour because one join key dominates the data: most tasks finish in seconds while a handful churn on a giant partition. Which TWO techniques most directly relieve this data skew?

options.A: Set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to 1 so all data lands in one task
options.B: Broadcast the larger of the two tables to every executor
options.C: Rely on Adaptive Query Execution to split the skewed partitions at runtime
options.D: Add a salt column to the skewed key and join on the salted keys
options.E: Persist both DataFrames with .cache() before the join

questions.explanation: AQE detects and splits oversized skewed partitions at runtime, and salting spreads the hot key across many reducers; both directly target the skew. Broadcasting only helps when the broadcast side is small (the large table is not a candidate), forcing a single shuffle partition makes skew worse, and caching does not change partition balance.

questions.question: A user holds the Viewer role on a workspace that contains a warehouse. With no additional item-level grants, what can that user do with the warehouse by default?

options.A: Create and drop tables in the warehouse
options.B: Add other users to the workspace as Viewers
options.C: Read data through Spark and the OneLake file APIs
options.D: Connect to the SQL endpoint and read data, subject to SQL-level permissions

questions.explanation: Viewer grants Read and ReadData, which lets the user connect to the SQL/TDS endpoint and read data (still bounded by the warehouse's own SQL permissions). Viewer does not include ReadAll, so reading through Spark or OneLake APIs is not permitted, and Viewer cannot create objects or manage membership.

questions.question: A Real-Time Dashboard tile must show the average temperature per sensor over the last 15 minutes and refresh automatically. The Telemetry table has columns Timestamp, SensorId, and Temperature. Which KQL backs the tile correctly?

options.A: Telemetry | where Timestamp > ago(15m) | summarize avg(Temperature) by SensorId
options.B: Telemetry | where Timestamp > ago(15m) | summarize avg(Temperature)
options.C: Telemetry | summarize avg(Temperature) by SensorId | where Timestamp > ago(15m)
options.D: Telemetry | top 15 by Timestamp | summarize avg(Temperature)

questions.explanation: Filtering first with where Timestamp > ago(15m), then summarize avg(Temperature) by SensorId, yields one average per sensor over the window, and the tile's auto-refresh re-runs it. Summarizing without 'by SensorId' collapses to a single overall average instead of one per sensor, placing the time filter after summarize fails because Timestamp no longer exists in the aggregated result, and using top 15 by Timestamp keeps only 15 rows rather than the full 15-minute window.

questions.question: Analysts comfortable with Power Query need to build reusable transformation logic (merges, joins, column shaping) that writes results into a lakehouse table and runs on Fabric capacity. Which item fits, and how does it relate to a data pipeline?

options.A: A KQL queryset, because it executes Power Query M against lakehouse tables
options.B: A Dataflow Gen2, which transforms with Power Query (M); a pipeline orchestrates and can invoke the dataflow but is not itself the transformation engine
options.C: A notebook, because Power Query transformations only run inside Spark notebooks
options.D: A data pipeline, because pipeline Copy activities expose Power Query transformations

questions.explanation: Dataflow Gen2 provides the low-code Power Query (M) authoring experience and can write to a lakehouse destination, which matches Power Query-savvy analysts. A data pipeline orchestrates and moves data and can call a dataflow or notebook, but the transformation logic lives in the dataflow, not in the Copy activity.

questions.question: An Eventhouse holds 90 days of telemetry (retention/SoftDeletePeriod = 90d). Most queries only touch the last 7 days, and you want to lower cost while keeping the full 90 days queryable. Which policy change achieves this, and what is the binding constraint?

options.A: Set delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration to 7 days on the Eventhouse tables
options.B: Set the caching policy to 180 days; a longer cache window reduces cold reads
options.C: Set the caching (hot cache) policy to 7 days; the cache period must be less than or equal to the retention period
options.D: Disable the caching policy entirely so all 90 days stay queryable
options.E: Set the retention policy to 7 days; older data then falls out of the hot cache automatically

questions.explanation: Lowering the hot-cache period to 7 days keeps recent data on premium SSD (fast and cheaper to cache) while the remaining 83 days sit in standard storage but stay queryable; the cache period must be at or below the retention period. Reducing retention to 7 days would delete the older data entirely, and deletedFileRetentionDuration is a Delta property, not an Eventhouse policy.
